finding: Most classic thinking biases show no correlation with general cognitive ability; a smaller set of specific biases (denominator neglect, probability matching, belief bias, matching bias) do correlate with cognitive ability.

Activating attachment to a primary caregiver in response to existential threat reduced defensive worldview endorsement and increased self-worth; the reliance on caregiver versus alternative relationships varied by an individual's attachment security.

Self-report consistency and differentiation across personality domains increased with age from childhood to adulthood, with different personality dimensions showing different trajectories of coherence and domain differentiation.

Deviations aligned with desirable group norms prompted greater willingness to express opinions and display pride compared to deviations misaligned with those norms, mediated by perceived conformity superiority.

Individuals who complied with a norm disliked those who refused to comply on principle; this rejection was mediated by perceived threat to the complier's positive self-image, and mitigated when a salient value was activated beforehand.

Upward counterfactual thinking protected self-esteem following failure when it identified an excuse, but reduced subsequent preparation and performance; the affective and motivational consequences of counterfactual thoughts depended on whether they served as excuses.

Lay intuition that tempting fate increases negative-outcome likelihood was driven by heightened accessibility of negative prospects following fate-tempting actions, mediated by the use of accessibility as a likelihood cue.

Expressing emotional vulnerability to a relational partner triggered a belief that the partner viewed the expresser as vulnerable, which reduced trust in the partner's positive regard and perpetuated subsequent vulnerability expression.
Marital quality and stability change over time in patterns explained by integrated theoretical models; longitudinal designs reveal trajectories masked by cross-sectional data.

Neuroticism was the strongest personality predictor of life satisfaction and negative affect; Extraversion and Agreeableness equally predicted positive affect; personality traits overall predicted emotional well-being more strongly than cognitive well-being.

Trait and state negative affect showed stronger correlations with job burnout dimensions and turnover intentions than positive affect; positive affect uniquely contributed to job satisfaction and organizational commitment alongside negative affect.

Stimulus normality is evaluated via comparison to norms constructed ad hoc from retrieved similar experiences and counterfactual alternatives, rather than against precomputed expectations; abnormal causes enhance emotional response.

Hindsight bias occurs for scientific results: people rate experimental outcomes as more predictable after learning the result; this effect is reduced by explicit consideration of alternative outcomes.

Reliance on feeling produced value as a step function of stimulus magnitude (insensitive to scope beyond presence/absence), whereas reliance on calculation produced near-linear value; the processing mode determined the shape of subjective value.

Low-probability events were mentally represented at higher abstraction levels than high-probability events, manifesting as broader categorization, preference for general descriptions, and reduced attention to specific details.

Initial exertion of executive control (attention, inhibition, working memory, emotion regulation) temporarily depleted capacity for subsequent executive control; prior demands on a limited control resource undermined later control performance.

Anticipation of emotional events evoked more intense emotion than retrospection about equivalent events; greater mental simulation of future events mediated the heightened evocativeness of anticipation.
Metacognitive experiences of difficulty or disfluency (degraded fonts, hard-to-read text, furrowed brow) activated analytic reasoning that reduced reliance on intuitive heuristics and defaults.
Short-term memory for order is driven partly by timing information; timing signals predict recall timing across both serial-order and timing-recall tasks.

Apparent absence of repetition blindness for nonwords resulted from participant use of partial orthographic information to guess repetitions; when guessing was made detectable, repetition blindness appeared for both words and nonwords.
